title: The Difference Between Forgiveness and Freedom
researcherTopic: polyvagal theory, neuroception, the social engagement system
metaDescription: Distinguish between the cognitive act of forgiveness and the somatic state of freedom in healing C-PTSD. Learn to reclaim your nervous system from the past.
imageDescription: A delicate piece of pottery, shattered and then meticulously repaired with shimmering gold, rests on a rustic wooden surface. Warm, gentle morning light illuminates the golden seams, highlighting the beauty of its restoration and resilience.
finalH2: From Forgiveness to Freedom: A Shift in Gaze
htmlContent:
I've sat with hundreds of people as they wrestle with the ghost of forgiveness, this pervasive cultural mandate that suggests peace is only available once you have cognitively absolved those who harmed you. Yet, what the long, quiet work of healing from complex trauma reveals is something far more nuanced and profoundly more liberating: the recognition that forgiveness is a cognitive act, while freedom is a somatic state, and the two are not nearly as related as we have been led to believe. The journey isn't from the cognitive shoreline of understanding to the deep, cellular territory of the body, is not about finding a reason to pardon, but about reclaiming the nervous system from the echoes of the past.

The Cognitive Trap of Forgiveness

We are creatures of narrative, and the story of forgiveness is one of the most deeply embedded in our collective psyche. It is presented as a moral and psychological imperative, a final developmental milestone in the journey of healing. We are told that to hold onto anger or resentment is to drink poison and expect the other person to die, a pithy and compelling aphorism that unfortunately collapses under the weight of complex trauma. This model presupposes a transactional reality, where an act of cognitive grace—the decision to forgive—somehow recalibrates the deep, non-verbal archives of the nervous system. But the body, as we know, keeps its own score, and it does not speak the language of shoulds and oughts. It speaks in the primal dialect of sensation, of activation, of threat and safety. To demand that a system still braced for impact simply decide to be at peace is a fundamental misunderstanding of how trauma reorganizes our entire being. It places the burden of resolution on the very part of us, the thinking mind, that is often the least reliable in the face of overwhelming experience. The intellect can build beautiful, elaborate justifications for why forgiveness is the right and noble path, while the amygdala continues to sound the alarm, the heart continues to race, and the gut continues to clench. This creates a painful and confusing dissonance, a state of internal fragmentation where one part of the self is shaming another for failing to achieve a state it is not yet equipped to inhabit. It becomes another stick with which to beat oneself, another metric of failure in a life already defined by a sense of not measuring up. The pursuit of forgiveness can, in this way, become a subtle form of self-abandonment, a turning away from the raw, unvarnished truth of our own somatic reality in favor of a more palatable, socially acceptable story.

We are not our thoughts, but we are responsible for our relationship to them.

The Body as the Arbiter of Freedom

The entire conversation shifts when we move our attention from the courthouse of the mind to the wild, untamed landscape of the body. Freedom, in the context of C-PTSD, is not a verdict delivered by a jury of our peers or our own intellect; it is a felt sense. It is the palpable experience of being able to take a full breath, of feeling one’s feet on the ground, of sensing the boundaries of one’s own skin without a concurrent sense of dread. This is the domain of the autonomic nervous system, the intricate, unconscious network that is constantly scanning the environment for cues of safety and danger. It is here that the work of liberation truly happens. The pioneering work of researcher Stephen Porges and his Polyvagal Theory provides a map for this territory, explaining how our physiological state dictates our psychological experience. Porges introduced the concept of neuroception, the nervous system’s capacity to assess risk without the involvement of the conscious mind. For the survivor of complex trauma, this neuroceptive process is often exquisitely sensitized, biased toward detecting threat even in its absence. The echoes of past danger become the lens through which the present is perceived. Freedom, then, is not about changing the past or forgiving the actors in it, but about updating this neuroceptive lens. It is about providing the nervous system with enough new, countervailing experiences of safety that it can begin to release its hypervigilant grip. This is not a cognitive process. You cannot talk your vagus nerve into a state of calm. As one of the most respected resources on trauma, the community at Psychology Today often highlights, the path to regulation is experiential. It is found in the rhythm of the breath, in the warmth of a compassionate gaze, in the grounding force of gravity. It is a slow, patient titration of presence, a gradual re-inhabiting of a body that was once a site of terror. The goal is not to forgive the unforgivable, but to become so rooted in the safety of the present moment that the past loses its power to hijack the nervous system.

When 'Letting Go' Becomes Another Burden

The injunction to “let go” is perhaps one of the most frustrating and invalidating pieces of advice offered to those healing from deep wounds. It implies that the past is something we are actively holding, a suitcase we could simply choose to put down if we were only enlightened or willing enough. For someone with a history of complex trauma, the past is not an object being held; it is a physiological state being re-enacted. The body remembers, not as a conscious choice, but as an automatic, protective response. The clenched jaw, the shallow breath, the ever-present tension in the shoulders—these are not choices. They are the somatic echoes of a time when these responses were necessary for survival. To suggest that one should simply “let go” of them is to fundamentally misunderstand the nature of traumatic memory, which is stored not as a coherent narrative but as fragmented sensory and emotional imprints. These imprints are not subject to the commands of the will. They operate beneath the level of conscious thought, driving reactions and shaping perceptions long after the original events have passed. In my years of working in this territory, I’ve seen how this pressure to “let go” becomes another layer of shame. It creates a narrative of personal failure: “If I were stronger, more spiritual, or more healed, I would be able to release this.” This transforms the natural, biological process of trauma recovery into a moral failing. The real work is not about letting go, but about learning to be with. It is about cultivating the capacity to stay present with the discomfort, the activation, and the grief without being consumed by it. It is the practice of acknowledging the reality of the suitcase—its weight, its texture, its history—without judgment. It is only through this radical act of being with what is that the nervous system can finally receive the message that the threat has passed, that the suitcase can, in its own time, be set down, not because we forced it, but because we are finally safe enough to do so. True freedom is not a forceful act of release, but the gentle, emergent property of a system that has returned to a state of regulation and trust. It’s a process beautifully articulated in the journey of healing a fragmented self, where integration, not amputation, is the goal.

The contemplative traditions all point to the same thing: what you're looking for is what's looking.

The Architecture of True Neutrality

In our cultural obsession with happiness and positivity, we have come to misunderstand the true nature of peace. We imagine it as a state of perpetual bliss, a constant feeling of lightness and joy. But for the nervous system, the ultimate state of safety is not positivity; it is neutrality. It is the absence of the alarm, the cessation of the hypervigilant scanning, the simple, unadorned experience of the present moment without the overlay of past or future. This is the promised land of healing. It is not a place of forced smiles or manufactured optimism, but a quiet, grounded state of being where the body is no longer at war with itself. This neutrality is the hallmark of a regulated ventral vagal state, the branch of our nervous system responsible for social engagement and connection. When this system is online, we are not necessarily “happy.” We are calm, we are present, we are open, we are available for connection. The world ceases to be a minefield of potential triggers and becomes, simply, the world. A car backfiring is just a sound, not a threat. A raised voice is just a communication, not a prelude to danger. This is a profound and radical shift, a move from a world colored by the constant threat of “what if” to a world grounded in the simple reality of “what is.” Achieving this state has nothing to do with forgiving the person who installed the minefield in the first place. It has everything to do with decommissioning the mines, one by one, through the patient, consistent application of awareness. It is the work of noticing the clench in the jaw and inviting it to soften, of noticing the shallow breath and allowing it to deepen, of noticing the impulse to flee and choosing to feel one’s feet on the floor instead. Each of these small, conscious acts is a vote for neutrality. It is a message sent to the deepest parts of the brain and body that the war is over, even if the memories of it remain. This is a more honest and attainable goal than the culturally imposed mandate of forgiveness. It does not ask us to lie to ourselves or to pretend that the harm did not matter. It asks only that we tend to our own internal landscape with enough care and precision that we can, at last, experience the profound peace of a system at rest. This journey is central to understanding the principles of somatic work.

The Practice of Somatic Uncoupling

Freedom from the past is not achieved through narrative reframing or cognitive bargaining; it is a physical process of uncoupling sensation from story. For years, or even decades, after a traumatic experience, a specific sensation—a tightness in the chest, a knot in the stomach, a wave of heat—can become inextricably linked to a story of terror, helplessness, or shame. The sensation arises, and the entire narrative of the trauma comes rushing in with it, hijacking the present moment and plunging the system back into a state of defense. The work of somatic uncoupling is the delicate, deliberate practice of separating these two elements. It is the process of learning to experience the raw, physical sensation on its own terms, without the accompanying story. This is profoundly challenging work, as the fusion of sensation and story is a powerful, deeply ingrained survival mechanism. It requires a willingness to approach the body with a quality of gentle, non-judgmental curiosity. A client once described this as learning to listen to the music without the lyrics. The music—the pure bodily sensation—might be intense, dissonant, or unpleasant. But without the lyrics of the traumatic narrative, it is just energy. It is just information moving through the system. There are many modalities that can support this process, from Somatic Experiencing to EMDR, a powerful tool for reprocessing traumatic memories that is well-documented by organizations like EMDRIA. The core principle of these approaches is the same: to allow the body to complete the responses that were interrupted at the time of the trauma, all while staying grounded in the safety of the present. This might look like allowing a wave of trembling to move through the limbs, or feeling a surge of anger without needing to act on it, or simply staying with a profound sense of stillness. It is in this space, between the sensation and the story, that the nervous system learns that it is no longer T/here. It learns that the sensation can exist without the threat, that the echo of the past can move through the body without destroying the present. This uncoupling is the essence of somatic freedom. It is the moment the past becomes memory, rather than a living, breathing reality.

Sit with it long enough and even the worst feeling reveals its edges.

From Forgiveness to Freedom: A Shift in Gaze

Ultimately, the journey out of complex trauma requires a radical shift in gaze—away from the perpetrator, away from the past, and toward the intimate, immediate reality of one’s own internal world. The culture teaches us to look outward for resolution, to seek apologies, to grant pardons, to find closure through some form of transaction with the other. But the body teaches us that resolution is an inside job. It is not about the other person at all. It is about our relationship with ourselves. It is about whether we are willing to turn toward the parts of us that are still frozen in time, still braced for impact, and offer them the one thing they have never had: a safe and compassionate witness. Forgiveness, with its focus on the other, can be a distraction from this deeper, more essential work. It keeps our attention fixed on the external event, on the person who harmed us, on the injustice of it all. While these are valid and important realities to acknowledge, they are not the territory of healing. The territory of healing is the body. It is the nervous system. It is the here and now. The moment we shift our focus from “How can I forgive them?” to “How can I be with myself in this moment?” is the moment the entire landscape of recovery changes. The question of forgiveness may resolve itself in time, or it may not. It may become irrelevant. What matters is not whether we can cognitively absolve another, but whether we can somatically liberate ourselves. Can we learn to inhabit our own skin with a sense of ease and belonging? Can we learn to trust the signals of our own body? Can we build a life that is not a reaction to the past, but an expression of our own deepest truth? This is the work. This is the path. It is a path that leads not to the hollow victory of forgiveness, but to the earned, embodied reality of freedom. It is a path that requires us to understand the intricate dance of our own survival responses and to meet them not with judgment, but with profound respect and care. And for those navigating the complexities of dissociation, resources from the International Society for the Study of Trauma and Dissociation can be an invaluable guide. What happens when we stop demanding the story have a different ending and instead learn to write a new chapter from a place of inner safety?

This is not about forgetting what happened. It is about no longer allowing what happened to dictate what happens next. It is the slow, painstaking work of reclaiming your own life, one breath at a time. The past may have written the first several chapters, but it does not get to write the ending. The pen is in your hand now. So, what will you write?
